content.text: Mock audits are the closest simulation of a real SOC 2, ISO 42001, or internal control audit you can run without an auditor in the room. For agentic AI systems — where evidence artefacts include inference logs, guardrail activation reports, and prompt version histories — a structured mock audit cycle is the only reliable way to discover control gaps before they become audit findings. This document covers how to plan and execute mock audits, classify findings by severity, build a remediation register, and close the loop before the formal audit window.

attrs.alt: Mock audit cycle: 5 phases from kickoff through closure, with findings classified as Critical, Significant, Observation, or No Finding, feeding a central remediation register

content.text: What It Is

A mock audit is a structured internal rehearsal that mirrors the procedures a real auditor applies: scoping, evidence request lists, walkthroughs, sample testing, and findings classification. It is distinct from a self-assessment checklist because it is conducted by people who are not the control owners — an internal audit team, a compliance lead from a different product line, or a contracted readiness consultant.

For agentic AI systems, a mock audit covers the same Trust Services Criteria a SOC 2 auditor would examine — logical access (CC6), system monitoring (CC7), risk management (CC9), and processing integrity (PI) — but applies AI-specific test procedures: verifying that inference logs are tamper-resistant, that guardrail activation events are retained for the required period, that prompt version history is linked to deployment records, and that model card documentation is current.
Why It Matters

A SOC 2 qualified opinion requires that controls be both designed effectively and operating effectively across the audit period. Design effectiveness means the control, if followed, would prevent or detect the risk. Operating effectiveness means it was actually followed, consistently, throughout the period. Agentic AI systems introduce novel failure modes in both dimensions: a guardrail designed correctly may silently stop activating after a model update; an access control designed for human operators may not apply to an agent service account.

Mock audits run six to twelve weeks before the formal audit window allow remediation of critical findings under non-audit conditions — no auditor clock is ticking, evidence can be re-collected, and control owners have time to implement fixes. Teams that skip mock audits routinely encounter the same finding categories in real audits: missing evidence for the first two months of the period, guardrail configuration not version-controlled, and no documented management response to prior exceptions.
Benefits
Identifies both design and operating effectiveness gaps before they appear in the formal audit opinion.
Trains control owners to understand what auditors look for, reducing walkthrough time during the real audit.
Builds a remediation register that becomes evidence of management's commitment to continuous improvement.
Surfaces AI-specific evidence gaps (missing inference logs, unlinked prompt versions) that standard control frameworks do not test for.
Creates a repeatable, improving cycle: each annual mock audit benefits from the prior year's findings register.
Reduces auditor discovery time, which lowers the number of hours billed and the likelihood of scope expansion.
Trade-offs

Mock audits consume real engineering and compliance capacity. A thorough mock audit for an agentic AI system typically requires two to four weeks of effort across control owners, a compliance lead, and an internal reviewer. Organizations with lean teams may not be able to dedicate this capacity annually without reducing other compliance work. The alternative — a lighter-weight readiness review — catches fewer issues and gives false confidence when findings are missed.

Mock audit findings can also surface politically sensitive gaps — a control that the CISO believed was operating but was not, or a team that had been skipping a monthly review. Managing these findings requires careful communication so they drive remediation rather than blame assignment.
How It Works: The Five-Phase Mock Audit Cycle
Kickoff.

Define the audit scope: which TSC categories, which AI systems, and which controls are in scope. Produce a written scope statement and control list. Assign a mock auditor role (internal audit, peer team, or readiness consultant). Set the timeline: evidence collection window, walkthrough week, findings draft, management response, and closure.
Evidence Collection.

Control owners gather evidence for the test period. For agentic AI systems this includes: inference log exports for sampled dates, guardrail activation reports, prompt version history from the registry, access review completion records, vulnerability scan results, and change management tickets for model updates.
Testing.

The mock auditor executes test scripts: walkthroughs (observe the control in operation), inspection (review documents and logs), and re-performance (independently execute a control step to verify it produces the correct output). Sample selection follows auditor-style rules — typically 25 items for populations above 250, or a complete population for populations of five or fewer.
Findings Classification.

Each exception is classified by severity and root cause. The four classifications are Critical (control failure; audit opinion at risk), Significant (design gap; likely management letter), Observation (enhancement opportunity; no audit impact), and No Finding (control is effective; evidence is sufficient).
Remediation and Closure.

Each finding enters the remediation register. The control owner provides a management response (agreed or disagree, with rationale). The mock auditor verifies remediation evidence before marking a finding closed. Critical findings require re-testing of the specific control before the formal audit window opens.
Finding Severity Definitions
Severity classification is the most consequential decision in the mock audit cycle. Misclassifying a significant finding as an observation delays remediation and creates audit risk.
Critical:

The control did not operate at all for a material portion of the audit period, or a design flaw means the control could never prevent or detect the targeted risk. Remediation SLA: 5 business days. Must be resolved before the formal audit window opens.
Significant:

The control operated most of the time but with exceptions that a real auditor would note in a management letter. Typically a design gap that allows the control to be bypassed, or operating gaps affecting more than 5% of the sampled population. Remediation SLA: 15 business days.
Observation:

The control is effective but could be strengthened. No impact on audit opinion. Includes items like lack of automation for a manual control, or incomplete documentation that is not yet causing failures. Remediation SLA: 30 business days.
No Finding:
The control is designed correctly and was operating effectively throughout the sampled period. Documented as evidence of control maturity, not just the absence of a problem.
Architecture: The Remediation Register

The remediation register is the central artefact of the mock audit cycle. It must be maintained in a system of record — a GRC platform, a JIRA project with a custom schema, or a governed Notion database — not a shared spreadsheet. Each row represents one finding and carries the following fields:
Finding ID (MOCK-YYYY-NNN format), Control ID, TSC category, and finding title.
Severity classification, root cause category (missing evidence, design gap, operating gap, documentation gap), and detailed description.
Control owner, management response (agree / disagree with rationale), and remediation plan.
Target remediation date, actual closure date, and status (Open / In Progress / Closed / Accepted Risk).
Evidence of closure: link to pull request, configuration change ticket, or re-test result.
For AI-specific findings: inference log export reference, guardrail configuration hash, prompt registry version at time of finding.
How to Implement: Running the Mock Audit
Step 1: Build the Test Script Library

A test script specifies, for each control: the test objective, the evidence to collect, the test procedure (walkthrough, inspection, or re-performance), the expected result, and the pass/fail criteria. AI-specific test scripts must cover inference log retention, guardrail activation monitoring, and prompt version traceability.

# mock-audit-test-script.yaml
# Control: CC7.2 - Agentic AI Inference Log Retention

test_id: MOCK-2025-CC7-001
control_id: CC7.2
tsc_category: CC7
test_objective: >
  Verify that inference logs for all agentic AI agents are retained for
  a minimum of 90 days and are accessible for auditor review.

evidence_required:
  - Inference log export for 3 sampled dates (one per month of period)
  - Log retention policy document (version current as of audit period start)
  - Access log showing compliance team can query inference logs
  - Screenshot of log storage configuration showing retention setting

test_procedure: inspection
test_steps:
  - step: 1
    action: Request inference log export for sampled date 1
    expected: Log file present; contains agent_id, timestamp, input_hash, output_hash, tool_calls
  - step: 2
    action: Verify log timestamp range covers the full sampled date
    expected: Logs present from 00:00:00 to 23:59:59 UTC
  - step: 3
    action: Confirm log storage bucket retention policy
    expected: Retention set to >= 90 days; deletion protection enabled
  - step: 4
    action: Verify compliance team member can access logs without engineering assistance
    expected: Access log shows successful query by compliance role

pass_criteria: All 4 steps produce expected results for all 3 sampled dates
fail_criteria: Any sampled date missing, any log field absent, retention < 90 days

ai_specific_fields:
  prompt_version_in_log: required
  guardrail_activation_events: required
  tool_permission_scope: required
Step 2: Classify and Document Findings

# classify_finding.py
# Severity classification helper for mock audit findings

from dataclasses import dataclass, field
from enum import Enum
from datetime import date, timedelta
from typing import Optional


class Severity(Enum):
    CRITICAL = "Critical"
    SIGNIFICANT = "Significant"
    OBSERVATION = "Observation"
    NO_FINDING = "No Finding"


class RootCause(Enum):
    MISSING_EVIDENCE = "Missing Evidence"
    DESIGN_GAP = "Design Gap"
    OPERATING_GAP = "Operating Gap"
    DOCUMENTATION_GAP = "Documentation Gap"


@dataclass
class Finding:
    finding_id: str
    control_id: str
    tsc_category: str
    title: str
    description: str
    severity: Severity
    root_cause: RootCause
    owner: str
    management_response: str
    remediation_plan: str
    target_date: date
    # AI-specific fields
    inference_log_ref: Optional[str] = None
    guardrail_config_hash: Optional[str] = None
    prompt_version: Optional[str] = None
    status: str = "Open"
    closure_evidence: Optional[str] = None
    actual_close_date: Optional[date] = None

    @property
    def sla_days(self) -> int:
        sla_map = {
            Severity.CRITICAL: 5,
            Severity.SIGNIFICANT: 15,
            Severity.OBSERVATION: 30,
            Severity.NO_FINDING: 0,
        }
        return sla_map[self.severity]

    @property
    def sla_date(self) -> date:
        return date.today() + timedelta(days=self.sla_days)

    def to_register_row(self) -> dict:
        return {
            "finding_id": self.finding_id,
            "control_id": self.control_id,
            "severity": self.severity.value,
            "title": self.title,
            "root_cause": self.root_cause.value,
            "owner": self.owner,
            "target_date": str(self.target_date),
            "sla_date": str(self.sla_date),
            "status": self.status,
            "ai_fields": {
                "inference_log_ref": self.inference_log_ref,
                "guardrail_config_hash": self.guardrail_config_hash,
                "prompt_version": self.prompt_version,
            },
        }


# Example: classify a critical finding for missing inference logs
finding = Finding(
    finding_id="MOCK-2025-001",
    control_id="CC7.2",
    tsc_category="CC7",
    title="Inference logs missing for 2025-01",
    description=(
        "Inference log export for January 2025 is not available. "
        "Log storage bucket was misconfigured from 2025-01-01 to 2025-01-12, "
        "resulting in 12 days of missing logs for all agents in the prod environment."
    ),
    severity=Severity.CRITICAL,
    root_cause=RootCause.MISSING_EVIDENCE,
    owner="platform-team@company.com",
    management_response="Agreed. Storage misconfiguration has been corrected. Implementing alerting for bucket health.",
    remediation_plan="Configure bucket health alert; validate daily log delivery via automated check.",
    target_date=date(2025, 3, 10),
    inference_log_ref="gs://prod-inference-logs/2025-01/",
    guardrail_config_hash="a3f9e2b1",
    prompt_version="v2.4.1",
)

print(finding.to_register_row())
Step 3: Automate Remediation Status Tracking

#!/usr/bin/env bash
# remediation-status-report.sh
# Generate weekly remediation status report from GRC platform CSV export

set -euo pipefail

REGISTER_CSV="${1:-remediation_register.csv}"
REPORT_DATE=$(date +%Y-%m-%d)
OVERDUE_COUNT=0
CRITICAL_OPEN=0

echo "=== Mock Audit Remediation Status Report ==="
echo "Report date: ${REPORT_DATE}"
echo ""

# Parse register and check SLA compliance
while IFS=',' read -r finding_id severity owner target_date status; do
  # Skip header row
  [[ "${finding_id}" == "finding_id" ]] && continue

  # Check for overdue open findings
  if [[ "${status}" == "Open" || "${status}" == "In Progress" ]]; then
    if [[ "${target_date}" < "${REPORT_DATE}" ]]; then
      echo "OVERDUE: ${finding_id} (${severity}) - Owner: ${owner}, Target: ${target_date}"
      ((OVERDUE_COUNT++)) || true
    fi
    if [[ "${severity}" == "Critical" ]]; then
      ((CRITICAL_OPEN++)) || true
    fi
  fi
done < "${REGISTER_CSV}"

echo ""
echo "Summary:"
echo "  Overdue findings: ${OVERDUE_COUNT}"
echo "  Open critical findings: ${CRITICAL_OPEN}"

if [[ ${CRITICAL_OPEN} -gt 0 ]]; then
  echo ""
  echo "ACTION REQUIRED: ${CRITICAL_OPEN} critical finding(s) remain open."
  echo "Escalate to CISO if not resolved within 5 business days."
  exit 1
fi
Examples: AI-Specific Finding Patterns
Agentic AI systems generate finding patterns that do not appear in traditional software audits. The most common categories encountered in practice:
Inference log gaps.
Log storage misconfiguration, rate limiting causing dropped events, or log rotation that overwrites records before the 90-day minimum retention period.
Prompt version traceability.
Agent deployed with a prompt version that is not recorded in the deployment manifest, making it impossible to reconstruct what prompt was in use for a given inference.
Guardrail drift.
Guardrail rules updated outside the change management process, with no ticket linking the configuration change to an approval.
Agent service account access review.
Quarterly access review process covers human accounts but excludes agent service accounts, which hold elevated permissions to call external tools.
Model card staleness.
Model card documents the model version from the initial deployment but has not been updated to reflect a subsequent fine-tune, which changed the model's risk profile.
Real-World Example: FinTech Agent Payments Platform

A FinTech company running an agentic AI payment reconciliation system conducted their first mock audit eight weeks before their annual SOC 2 Type II audit. The mock auditor, drawn from the internal audit team, ran a CC6 logical access test on the agent service account and discovered it held write access to the production payments database — a permission that had been granted during a debugging session and never revoked.

This was classified as Critical (design gap: service account had more access than required by its function). The finding was remediated in three days: the service account was restricted to read-only access, a quarterly service account access review process was implemented and documented, and the change was linked to a JIRA ticket with CTO approval. When the real SOC 2 auditor examined CC6, the control was effective and the management response to the prior finding was already in the remediation register, demonstrating proactive risk management.
Common Mistakes
Self-auditing.
Control owners testing their own controls produces optimistic results. The mock auditor must be independent of the control operation.
Scope that is too narrow.
Limiting the mock audit to the controls the team is confident about and skipping the ones they are uncertain about is selection bias that defeats the purpose.
Closing findings with compensating controls that do not address the root cause.
A missing inference log finding is not closed by saying "our engineers can reconstruct the log from memory". It requires the log to exist.
No management response for accepted-risk findings.

Every finding, including those accepted rather than remediated, requires a written management response with a rationale. An auditor finding an unacknowledged gap is worse than a documented acceptance.
Running the mock audit too close to the formal audit window.
Critical findings require five business days to remediate and then re-testing. Starting six weeks before the audit window leaves no buffer.
Not including AI-specific test procedures.
Using a generic SOC 2 test script library without adapting for inference logs, guardrail activation, and prompt versioning will miss the most common agentic AI control gaps.
Best Practices
Schedule the mock audit kickoff exactly 12 weeks before the formal audit window opens. This leaves time for two rounds of remediation if a critical finding recurs after the initial fix.

Use the same evidence request list format your external auditor uses. If you have a prior year's PBC (provided by client) list, adapt it for the mock audit. This trains control owners on the exact artefacts the auditor will request.

Maintain the remediation register in your GRC platform, not a spreadsheet. Vanta, Drata, and ServiceNow GRC all support custom finding workflows that enforce management response requirements and track evidence of closure.
For AI-specific controls, verify evidence using hash-based integrity checks rather than visual inspection. An inference log that has been modified after the fact is not valid evidence.

Produce a mock audit summary report with a findings heatmap that can be presented to the board or audit committee. This demonstrates that AI risk management is a governed program, not a reactive exercise.
Performance and Scalability

As the number of agentic AI systems in scope grows, manual evidence collection becomes the bottleneck. Teams with more than five distinct agents should automate evidence packaging: a daily job exports inference log statistics, guardrail activation counts, and access review status to a shared evidence store that both control owners and mock auditors can access.

The remediation register itself must scale. A single register covering all controls for all systems works up to roughly 150 controls. Beyond that, segment registers by TSC category or by AI system, with a rollup view for the CISO and audit committee.
Tooling and Ecosystem
GRC Platforms:
Vanta and Drata both support custom controls and finding workflows. ServiceNow GRC is the enterprise option for organizations with existing ServiceNow deployments.
Evidence Storage:
S3 or GCS with object lock for tamper-resistant inference log storage. Evidence packages should use SHA-256 manifests.
Issue Tracking:
JIRA with a custom "Audit Finding" issue type allows finding IDs to link directly to remediation tickets, creating a clear chain of evidence from gap to closure.
Automation:

Python scripts can automate evidence packaging, SLA breach alerts, and register export for auditor consumption. Integrate with your CI/CD pipeline to attach deployment manifests (including prompt versions) to each production release.
Comparison with Alternatives
Approach
Coverage
Independence
AI-Specific
Effort
Full mock audit
All in-scope controls
High (independent reviewer)
Yes (custom test scripts)
High (2–4 weeks)
Self-assessment checklist
Selected controls
Low (control owners)
Only if adapted
Low (1–2 days)
Readiness review (consultant)
Risk-based subset
High (external)
Depends on consultant
Medium (1–2 weeks)
Continuous monitoring
Automated controls only
N/A (tooling)
Platform-dependent
Low (ongoing)
Security and Reliability

The remediation register itself is an audit artefact. It must be stored in a system with access controls (only compliance team, auditors, and named control owners can read or modify), an immutable audit trail of all changes, and retention consistent with your document retention policy (typically seven years for SOC 2 evidence).

Evidence packages for AI-specific findings must include integrity proofs. A SHA-256 manifest of each log file included in evidence ensures that an auditor — or a future regulator — can verify the logs were not modified after collection. Store manifests alongside the evidence in object-locked storage.

Never store raw inference logs — which may contain PII or confidential user inputs — in the remediation register. Store only the log reference (bucket path and manifest hash). The auditor requests access to the full log through a governed process, not by reading the register.
Mental Model

Think of the mock audit as a dress rehearsal for a performance where the stakes are your SOC 2 opinion. The dress rehearsal is the only time you can stop and fix the costumes, the script, and the lighting without the audience in the seats. The remediation register is the stage manager's notes: every issue found in rehearsal, who is fixing it, and when it will be resolved before opening night. You do not go on stage hoping the issues fixed themselves.
Key Takeaways
A mock audit is a structured rehearsal that tests both design effectiveness and operating effectiveness of controls, run by someone independent of the control operation.
The four severity classifications — Critical, Significant, Observation, No Finding — drive remediation SLAs: 5, 15, 30, and 0 business days respectively.
The remediation register is the central artefact: every finding requires a finding ID, management response, remediation plan, target date, and evidence of closure.
Agentic AI systems introduce specific finding categories: inference log gaps, prompt version traceability, guardrail drift, agent service account access, and model card staleness.
Start the mock audit 12 weeks before the formal audit window to allow time for remediation and re-testing of critical findings.
Evidence integrity for AI-specific findings requires SHA-256 manifests stored in object-locked storage — visual inspection of logs is insufficient.
The remediation register itself must be governed: access-controlled, audit-trailed, and retained for the same period as formal audit evidence.
